metadata.description: Curated issues in the NH Revised Statutes, proof-of-concept starting with Titles LVIII-LXII.

categories.contradiction: Internal conflicts or drafting inconsistencies between sections.
categories.ambiguity: Vague or undefined terms that leave application unclear.
categories.outdated: References, dates, or framings that have not been modernized.
categories.discrimination: Discriminatory framings embedded in statutory language.

metadata.notes: Quotes are taken verbatim from the scraped HTML. Each `refs` entry points to the canonical section id in laws.json. Links to the official NH General Court site accompany each issue in the UI.

issues.title: Parental-discipline justification turns on undefined 'misconduct'
issues.description: RSA 627:6 permits a parent or guardian to use force against a minor when the adult 'reasonably believes it necessary to prevent or punish such minor's misconduct'. Neither 'misconduct' nor the force ceiling is defined in the statute, leaving broad discretion over what kinds of corporal discipline are lawful.
issues.quote: A parent, guardian or other person responsible for the general care and welfare of a minor is justified in using force against such minor when and to the extent that he reasonably believes it necessary to prevent or punish such minor's misconduct.

issues.title: 'Reasonable person' standard left undefined across Culpability chapter
issues.description: The core culpability definitions in RSA 626:2 build criminal negligence on the behaviour 'a reasonable person would observe in the situation', without defining what that person is, what characteristics they share with the defendant, or how the standard is applied. The phrase recurs throughout the code but is nowhere anchored.
issues.quote: ...a gross deviation from the conduct that a reasonable person would observe in the situation.

issues.title: 'Substantial risk' used as a threshold without further definition
issues.description: Entrapment (626:5) and the definition of deadly force (627:9) both hinge on whether conduct creates a 'substantial risk' of a particular outcome, but 'substantial' is not further qualified. Where the difference between deadly and non-deadly force triggers very different consequences, the undefined threshold is notable.
issues.quote: "Deadly force" means any assault or confinement which the actor commits with the purpose of causing or which he knows to create a substantial risk of causing death or serious bodily injury.

issues.title: Consent provision relies on vague 'reasonable judgment as to the harm involved'
issues.description: RSA 626:6 determines when consent negates an offence by asking whether the consenting party could exercise 'reasonable judgment as to the harm involved'. 'Reasonable judgment' is undefined and overlaps with but does not match the separate 'reasonable person' standard in 626:2, leaving unclear which test courts are meant to apply.
issues.quote: ...or to be unable to exercise a reasonable judgment as to the harm involved.

issues.title: Wiretap and eavesdropping chapter still governed by 1969 framework
issues.description: Core provisions in RSA 570-A (wiretapping and eavesdropping) carry effective dates from 1969 and 1976 with only light amendment since. The underlying technological context — landline telephony — bears little resemblance to modern internet, mobile, and cloud communications, so the statutory text leaves significant ambiguity around current surveillance technology.

issues.title: Indecent-exposure statute uses 'fornicates' without statutory definition
issues.description: RSA 645:1 criminalises one who 'fornicates, exposes his or her genitals, or performs any other act of gross lewdness' in public. 'Fornicates' is archaic language carried over from earlier moral-offence drafting and is nowhere defined in the Criminal Code, despite being the first element of the offence.
issues.quote: Such person fornicates, exposes his or her genitals, or performs any other act of gross lewdness under circumstances which he or she should know will likely cause affront or alarm...

issues.title: Unauthorized-use-of-firearms offence depends on undefined 'compact part of a town'
issues.description: RSA 644:13 makes it an offence to discharge a firearm 'within the compact part of a town or city', and then defines 'compact part' by enumeration of building types plus an adjustable perimeter. The result is a circular, highly fact-dependent test: whether conduct is a crime can turn on whether a particular building happens to qualify, with no central list or map.
issues.quote: A person is guilty of a violation if, within the compact part of a town or city, such person fires or discharges any cannon, gun, pistol, air rifle or other firearm...

issues.title: Insanity defence and prisoner-transfer statutes still use 'insane' as a legal category
issues.description: RSA 628:2 frames the insanity defence around a person who 'is insane at the time he acts', and RSA 651:10 and 651:11 authorise transfers of any 'insane' prisoner to the secure psychiatric unit. Elsewhere in the sentencing chapter (RSA 651:8-b) the same code uses the modern clinical formulation 'mental disease or defect', so the code applies two different vocabularies to the same concept, one of which is both outdated and stigmatising.
issues.quote: A person who is insane at the time he acts is not criminally responsible for his conduct.

issues.title: Spousal-privilege carve-out for sex-assault cases still framed as 'husband and wife'
issues.description: RSA 632-A:5 disapplies the spousal-communications privilege in sexual-assault prosecutions, but only uses the phrase 'communications between husband and wife'. Since the Supreme Court's 2015 recognition of same-sex marriage and NH's own 2010 marriage-equality statute, this phrasing is technically ambiguous as to whether the carve-out covers all legal spouses or only different-sex pairs.
issues.quote: Laws attaching a privilege against the disclosure of communications between husband and wife are inapplicable to proceedings under this chapter.

issues.title: Interstate corrections compacts still use the term 'mentally defective'
issues.description: Both the New England Interstate Corrections Compact (RSA 622-A:2) and the broader Interstate Corrections Compact (RSA 622-B:2) describe facilities 'for the mentally ill or mentally defective'. 'Mentally defective' is obsolete clinical language no longer used in medical or rights-based frameworks and carries a clearly pejorative tone.
issues.quote: ...a facility for the mentally ill or mentally defective) in which inmates as defined in (d) above may lawfully be confined...

issues.title: Criminal-procedure fees fixed in cents-era statutes never re-indexed to inflation
issues.description: RSA 592-A:3 still describes a threshold where 'the punishment is a fine of $100 or of some specific sum less than $100', RSA 592-A:15 caps certain refusals at $100, and RSA 597:20 sets a court-officer recognisance fee at $60. These amounts derive from 19th- and mid-20th-century drafting. Their purchasing power has fallen by an order of magnitude, yet the thresholds continue to steer jurisdiction and procedural routes.
issues.quote: ...in any case where the punishment is a fine of $100 or of some specific sum less than $100, or imprisonment for 6 months or less...

issues.title: General-definitions statute still expands 'insane' to include 'idiot', 'lunatic', 'non-compos' and 'distracted person'
issues.description: RSA 21:18, the statewide general-definitions chapter that binds every statute lacking its own definitions, defines 'insane' or 'insane person' to 'include every idiot, non-compos, lunatic, insane, or distracted person'. These are archaic moral/medical labels whose original drafting traces back to the Revised Statutes of 1842 (RS 1:15). Because this definition is still active, every statute that uses the word 'insane' inherits this slur-laden expansion.
issues.quote: The words "insane" or "insane person" shall include every idiot, non-compos, lunatic, insane, or distracted person.

issues.title: Communications statutes still enumerate the telegraph as a covered medium
issues.description: Core communications-and-services definitions in the Criminal Code (RSA 637:8 theft of services, RSA 649:1 criminal mischief definitions) and the wiretap chapter (RSA 570-A:1) still explicitly list 'telegraph' alongside telephone and radio. Public telegraph service ended in the United States in the early 2000s; the references now do nothing except leave modern, equivalent technologies (text messaging, push notifications, VoIP) ambiguously outside the list.
issues.quote: ...telephone or telegraph service, gas, electricity, water or steam, admission to entertainment...

issues.title: Tax, estate, and sexual-assault statutes still structured around 'husband and wife'
issues.description: Three unrelated chapters of the NH code still frame substantive rules around the phrase 'husband and wife' rather than the gender-neutral 'spouses'. RSA 72:31 is a tax-credit provision titled and drafted 'Husband and Wife'. RSA 551:13 defines when a separation decree does not terminate 'the status of husband and wife' for will-revocation purposes. RSA 632-A:5 carves out the husband-and-wife communications privilege in sexual-assault cases. The phrasing predates NH's 2010 marriage-equality statute and the 2015 federal recognition of same-sex marriage, and forces statutory interpreters to assume 'husband and wife' means 'any two spouses' without that assurance being written down.
issues.quote: A husband and wife, each qualifying for a tax credit, shall each be granted a tax credit upon their residential real estate...

issues.title: Mandatory $50 domestic-violence surcharge never indexed to inflation
issues.description: Every offence in the assault and sexual-assault chapters that can be recorded as 'domestic violence' carries a mandatory additional fine of exactly $50 on top of any other penalty. Although serious offences like first-degree assault and aggravated felonious sexual assault routinely involve penalties in the tens of thousands of dollars, the domestic-violence add-on has remained frozen at $50. Because the statute reads 'notwithstanding any other penalty authorized by law', the figure is not discretionary and cannot be inflated by the court.
issues.quote: Notwithstanding any other penalty authorized by law, the court shall levy a fine of $50 for each conviction recorded as "...-domestic violence" under this section. The court shall not reduce or suspend any sentence or fine imposed under this paragraph.

issues.title: Hundreds of still-operative sections trace unbroken to pre-1900 source dates
issues.description: A broad sweep of the scraped titles shows roughly 537 sections still carrying effective dates from the 19th century in their Source lines, with the earliest traces going back to the Revised Statutes of 1842 and compiled statutes of 1853 and 1867. While many have been amended over the years, numerous sections — especially in Titles I (State and Government), LVI (Probate), LIX (Criminal Procedure), and LXII (Criminal Code) — still carry original 19th-century drafting forward with only minor adjustments. Representative examples: RSA 21:18 (1842), 551:13 (1842), 592-A:1 (1855).

issues.title: 'Law enforcement officer' is defined inconsistently across five chapters
issues.description: At least five chapters define the phrase 'law enforcement officer' on their own terms. RSA 651:6 and 106-M:1 use a narrow enumeration (sheriff or deputy, state police, constable). RSA 106-L:2 gives an employment-based test (anyone employed by a local, municipal, county, or state government in specified roles). RSA 106-B:35 is the broadest, covering any state, county, or municipal law-enforcement officer. RSA 642:3-a outsources its definition to 'as defined in RSA 630:1, II'. Whether a particular person is a 'law enforcement officer' can therefore depend entirely on which chapter's definition the prosecution invokes, with no central cross-reference.
issues.quote: RSA 651:6: a sheriff or deputy sheriff of any county, a state police officer, a constable or a police officer... | RSA 106-B:35: any state, county, or municipal law enforcement officer.

issues.title: 'Inmate' defined in gendered and incompatible ways across multiple chapters
issues.description: The statutes define 'inmate' at least three different ways. The interstate-corrections compacts (RSA 622-A:2, 622-B:2) say 'a male or female offender who is committed, under sentence to or confined in a penal or correctional institution' — both gendered and confinement-specific. RSA 623-B:1 uses the broader 'a person confined to a correctional facility as the result of conviction of a crime'. RSA 21-H:2 says any 'adult committed by law to the custody of the commissioner'. The 'male or female' framing is additionally outdated given that NH law recognises non-binary gender elsewhere.
issues.quote: "Inmate" means a male or female offender who is committed, under sentence to or confined in a penal or correctional institution.

issues.title: 'Emancipated minor' statutorily defined as 'minor female' only
issues.description: The abortion-statute definitions chapter (RSA 132, under Public Health) defines 'emancipated minor' in RSA 132:32 as 'any minor female who is or has been married or has by court order otherwise been freed from the care, custody, and control of her parents'. Although the surrounding subdivision is about abortion services — which involve female patients — the general concept 'emancipated minor' is elsewhere applied to any minor irrespective of sex. Anchoring the statutory definition to 'minor female' therefore encodes a gender assumption into a common legal term.
issues.quote: "Emancipated minor" means any minor female who is or has been married or has by court order otherwise been freed from the care, custody, and control of her parents.

issues.title: Resident tax statute still exempts 'aliens' and 'insane persons' by name
issues.description: RSA 72:1, the 'Persons Liable' section that imposes the resident tax on every inhabitant 18 to 65, lists the exemptions as 'assisted persons, insane persons, the surviving spouse of any veteran...'. The categories read like a 19th-century poor-law carve-out: 'assisted persons' is archaic phrasing for people receiving public assistance, 'insane persons' is a stigmatising clinical label (and one that pulls in the expansive 21:18 definition covering 'idiot, lunatic, non-compos, distracted person'), and the section still uses 'alien' rather than the modern 'non-citizen'.
issues.quote: ...a tax of $10, to be known as the "resident tax," shall be assessed on every inhabitant of the state from 18 to 65 years of age whether a citizen of the United States or an alien, except assisted persons, insane persons, the surviving spouse of any veteran...

issues.title: 'Moral turpitude' used as a disqualifier across many statutes with no shared definition
issues.description: The phrase 'moral turpitude' (or 'immoral behavior') appears as a disqualifying ground in municipal-employment, military-appointment, and licensing statutes across NH. Only RSA 106-L:2 (police standards and training council) gives it a definition ('an illegal act involving dishonesty, deceit, theft, or willful misconduct') and only for that chapter. Every other chapter — e.g. RSA 28:10-a (county employment), RSA 47:17 (municipal ordinances), RSA 110-B:20 / 110-B:24 (New Hampshire National Guard commissioning and discharge) — leaves the phrase undefined, forcing agencies to improvise on a term already criticised in federal jurisprudence for its unconstitutional vagueness.
issues.quote: ...the commission of an offense involving moral turpitude or punishable by a court-martial, shall not be eligible for appointment as a commissioned officer...

issues.title: Licensing across unrelated professions turns on undefined 'good moral character'
issues.description: Several professional-licensing chapters gate admission on a finding of 'good moral character' without defining the phrase. Examples include police officers (RSA 106-L:6), healthcare-facility certification (RSA 151-A:5), employment-agency licensure (RSA 277-B:7), accounting firm ownership (RSA 309-B:8), and architecture licensure (RSA 310-A:170). The term is a legacy standard from early-20th-century licensing drafting; its open-texture leaves boards with substantial discretion to deny applications on grounds that cannot be crisply reviewed on appeal.
issues.quote: ...(c) Is of good moral character; and (d) Has a high school education, or the equivalent thereof, as defined by the department...

issues.title: Clergy-privilege statute enumerates only Christian and Jewish roles by name
issues.description: RSA 516:35, the statutory privilege excusing religious leaders from disclosing confessions, lists 'a priest, rabbi or ordained or licensed minister of any church or a duly accredited Christian Science practitioner'. Imams, swamis, lamas, rinpoches, babalawos, shamans, and non-theistic spiritual advisers are nowhere named and are brought in — if at all — only through a strained reading of 'minister of any church'. The marriage-solemnisation statute, RSA 457:31, picks a similar Christian frame by beginning with 'minister of the gospel'. The effect is that the state's default clergy-privilege roster reflects the religious demographics of NH in the mid-20th century, not today.
issues.quote: A priest, rabbi or ordained or licensed minister of any church or a duly accredited Christian Science practitioner shall not be required to disclose a confession or confidence made to him in his professional character as spiritual adviser...

issues.title: 'Satisfactory evidence' / 'satisfactory proof' used as a threshold without any definition
issues.description: The phrase 'satisfactory evidence', 'satisfactory proof', or 'satisfactory showing' appears in roughly 100 sections across the NH code as the trigger for agency or court action — for example RSA 21-I:35 (state-employee insurance eligibility), RSA 21-P:29 (firefighter certification), RSA 482-B:5 (well-contractor licensure), RSA 514:3 and 514:6 (service of process). It is nowhere defined, and NH case law treats it as a free-standing substantive standard distinct from both 'preponderance' and 'clear and convincing'. The result is a patchwork where the same language can mean very different things depending on the chapter.
issues.quote: ...upon satisfactory evidence that the order has been complied with, the notice shall be sufficient.

issues.title: 'Building' defined four incompatible ways across the code
issues.description: The single word 'building' is given materially different statutory definitions depending on which chapter you are in. RSA 153:1 (state fire code) defines it as 'any structure, framework or housing, public or private, excluding single family dwellings as defined in paragraph X'. RSA 155-A:1 (state building code) defines 'building' by reference to the International Building Code adopted under RSA 155-A. RSA 227-M:2 (forestry / cultural resources) defines 'building' anthropologically as 'a construction made by humans and created to shelter human activity'. RSA 479-A:1 (condominiums) requires 'a building or group of buildings having a total of 5 or more units'. The same physical structure can therefore be a 'building' for some statutory purposes and not for others, with no cross-reference reconciling them.
issues.quote: RSA 153:1: 'any structure, framework or housing... excluding single family dwellings...' | RSA 227-M:2: 'a construction made by humans and created to shelter human activity'.

issues.title: 'Dealer' defined inconsistently — and twice with overlapping subject matter
issues.description: At least five chapters give independent definitions of 'dealer'. RSA 637:7 (theft offences) calls a dealer 'a person in the business of buying or selling goods'. RSA 339-C:1 narrows it to gasoline retailers. RSA 358-F:1 narrows it to used-motor-vehicle retailers. The two off-highway vehicle / snowmobile chapters — RSA 215-A:1 and RSA 215-C:1 — each define 'dealer' for OHRV/snowmobile sales but in subtly different language, even though their subject matter overlaps. There is no master definition controlling, so whether someone is a 'dealer' subject to a regulatory scheme can depend on which RSA the prosecutor or agency invokes.
issues.quote: RSA 215-A:1: 'a person in the OHRV or snowmobile business who sells OHRVs to the general public...' | RSA 215-C:1: 'a person in the snowmobile or OHRV business who sells snowmobiles...'

issues.title: Arrest-procedure timing turns on undefined 'without unreasonable delay'
issues.description: RSA 594:19-a (detention of person arrested) and RSA 594:20-a (place and time of detention) require an officer who arrests a person without a warrant to bring them before a court 'without unreasonable delay', and elsewhere fix a 36-hour cap. Neither 'unreasonable' nor the relationship between the open-textured 'without unreasonable delay' standard and the 36-hour absolute limit is defined: a 24-hour delay could be 'unreasonable' under one chapter's facts and not under another's. The phrase appears 26 times across the code in similar mandatory-action contexts.
issues.quote: An officer arresting a person without a warrant shall without unreasonable delay take the accused to the court... All persons shall appear no later than 36 hours after arrest...

issues.title: Juvenile-justice and child-welfare decisions hinge on the undefined 'best interest of the child'
issues.description: Across child-welfare and juvenile-justice statutes, transfers, diversions, releases, and discharges all turn on whether a court or agency finds the action 'in the best interest of the child' (or minor, or obligee). Examples: RSA 169-B:5 (venue transfer for delinquency cases), RSA 169-B:10 (juvenile diversion), RSA 169-B:22-a (notice to school of out-of-home placement), RSA 161-B:4 (child-support payee changes), RSA 621:19 (releases by youth services board). The phrase is nowhere statutorily defined; courts apply ad-hoc multi-factor tests that vary by judge.
issues.quote: ...if the court finds upon the motion of any party that it is in the best interest of the child, obligee, or obligor to do so.

issues.title: 'Notwithstanding any other provision of law' used 400+ times, eroding statutory coherence
issues.description: The phrase 'notwithstanding any other provision of law' appears in roughly 416 sections across the NH code. Each occurrence functions as a wildcard override of the surrounding statutory framework — granting a single section primacy over an unbounded set of other rules without enumerating which ones. The cumulative effect is hard for any reader (citizen, lawyer, or agency) to map: knowing what RSA X says no longer tells you what is actually controlling, because some other RSA Y may be a 'notwithstanding' carve-out. Examples: RSA 4:6-a (secretary of state office space), RSA 4:9-a (memorial siting), RSA 4-A:4 (compensation rules), RSA 5:6-d (election fund). The pattern is most concentrated in budget, election, and tax statutes.
issues.quote: Notwithstanding any other provision of law to the contrary, the following shall be assigned for use by the secretary of state...

issues.title: Open-textured 'sufficient cause' is the trigger for very different remedies
issues.description: The same phrase 'sufficient cause' (or 'good and sufficient cause') functions as the trigger for incompatible kinds of action across the code: dismissal of city watchmen and constables (RSA 47:14), denial of a well-contractor's license (RSA 482-B:8), forfeiture of a juror's per-diem pay (RSA 500-A:19), late filing of a writ of error (RSA 508:7), discharge of a person on habeas corpus (RSA 534:22), and probate-court appearances by sick parties (RSA 547:20). The standard is nowhere defined; whether a particular reason is 'sufficient' depends entirely on the discretion of the deciding officer.
issues.quote: Any person who, without sufficient cause, neglects to attend court after having been selected as a juror and duly notified...

issues.title: Document-management statutes still authorize photostat, microfilm, and multilithing
issues.description: The state's general document-reproduction authority (RSA 20-A:1 and 20-A:5) authorises the duplication of forms, letters, papers and reports 'by mimeographing, photostating, multilithing, microfilming, or reproducing by any other similar methods'. RSA 5:30 directs the director of records to 'establish and maintain a central microfilming laboratory and establish micrographic standards for public records'. None of these terms describe technologies still in routine state use; modern equivalents are PDFs and cloud storage. The catch-all 'similar methods' is left to do all the modern work.
issues.quote: ...the duplication of forms, letters, papers, reports and the like by mimeographing, photostating, multilithing, microfilming, or reproducing by any other similar methods...

issues.title: Common-law writs (assumpsit, replevin, distraint) still operative as enforcement tools
issues.description: Several procedural and tax-collection chapters still rely on common-law writ vocabulary that long ago lost its independent meaning. The tax department may 'distrain the personal estate, property interest, right or credit' (RSA 21-J:28-d, RSA 80:2, RSA 80:5). Co-tenants and creditors may bring 'an action of assumpsit' (RSA 507:2, 507:3, 511:15). 'Replevin' is preserved as a special-procedure carve-out under RSA 511-A:1 and RSA 511:38. Modern courts treat these forms as merged into general civil procedure, but the statutory text still requires litigants and tax officials to know the historical writ system to navigate the language.
issues.quote: The collector may distrain the goods, chattels, personal estate, property interest, right, or credit of such person or corporation...

issues.title: 'Forthwith' as a timing standard 200+ times — never defined
issues.description: The word 'forthwith' appears in roughly 214 sections as a mandatory timing trigger — typically directing an officer or official to act 'forthwith' upon some event. It is nowhere defined in the code. Black's Law Dictionary distinguishes 'forthwith' from 'immediately' but NH case law treats them interchangeably. The result is that statutes commanding action 'forthwith' (e.g. RSA 4:26 directing arrest of a parole violator, RSA 4:27 directing return to the institution, RSA 16:3 directing the secretary of state to mail notice) provide no fixed window for compliance.
issues.quote: ...the warden of the institution in which the prisoner was confined shall forthwith cause him to be arrested...

issues.title: 'Permanent' defined two materially different ways across chapters
issues.description: The single word 'permanent' carries different statutory definitions in unrelated chapters. The firefighter service-award program (RSA 100-B:2) defines 'permanent' as 'an individual regularly employed on a full-time basis by any fire department'. The Clean Air Act emissions-reduction chapter (RSA 125-J:1) defines 'permanent' as 'that the emissions reductions implemented for the generation and certification of an emission reduction credit are permanent'. The two definitions overlap only in being labelled 'permanent'; otherwise they describe entirely different concepts. A reader who relies on one chapter's definition while reading the other will be misled.
issues.quote: RSA 100-B:2: '"Permanent" means an individual regularly employed on a full-time basis by any fire department.' | RSA 125-J:1: '"Permanent" means that the emissions reductions implemented for the generation and certification of an emission reduction credit are permanent.'

issues.title: 'Court of competent jurisdiction' used 280+ times as a circular reference
issues.description: The phrase 'court of competent jurisdiction' appears roughly 288 times across the NH code as the body that is supposed to take some defined action — for example RSA 5-C:24 paternity petitions, RSA 5-C:28 paternity rescissions, RSA 5-C:32 adoption recordings, RSA 5-C:33 birth-record amendments. The phrase is essentially circular: the court is competent if it has jurisdiction, and it has jurisdiction if it is competent. The code never enumerates what the controlling test is, leaving a citizen to consult procedural rules and case law to find the relevant court.
issues.quote: Such request shall be sent in the form of petition to a court of competent jurisdiction; and, if the court approves the request...

issues.title: Misdemeanor classification rules in 625:9 are not consistently followed elsewhere
issues.description: RSA 625:9 sets up the classification system for misdemeanors: every misdemeanor is presumed to be class B unless the state files notice or specific elements apply. But other chapters write classification directly into substantive offences in ways that bypass the 625:9 default — for example RSA 638:1 declares 'Forgery is a class A misdemeanor if the writing is or purports to be a fake or counterfeit certificate of insurance' and 'all other forgery is a class B misdemeanor', without invoking the 625:9 'state files notice' procedure. The bail statute RSA 597:2 also draws lines between class A and class B misdemeanors for pre-trial detention without re-tracing the 625:9 logic. The classification system is therefore consistent on its face but inconsistent in practice.
issues.quote: RSA 625:9: 'Any crime designated within or outside this code as a misdemeanor without specification of the classification shall be presumed to be a class B misdemeanor unless... The state files a notice of intent to seek class A misdemeanor penalties on or before the date of arraignment.'

issues.title: 'Speedy trial' invoked as a duty without statutory time bound
issues.description: Several chapters require the state to ensure or secure a 'speedy trial' but never define how long is too long. RSA 632-A:9 directs the prosecutor in sexual-assault cases to 'take appropriate action to ensure a speedy trial to minimize the length of time the victim must endure the stress of involvement in the proceedings'. RSA 606-A:1 (interstate agreement on detainers) acknowledges 'difficulties in securing speedy trial of persons already incarcerated in other jurisdictions'. RSA 490:6-a allows emergency suspension of 'speedy trial procedures'. The absence of a fixed window means the constitutional right is the only working backstop.
issues.quote: ...shall take appropriate action to ensure a speedy trial to minimize the length of time the victim must endure the stress of involvement in the prosecution.

issues.title: 'Vested rights' recognised by statute but defined only by self-reference
issues.description: Multiple chapters carve out exceptions for 'vested rights' but the term is defined only by paraphrase. RSA 4:39-d (state-real-estate leasing) and RSA 228:57-a (railroad-corridor leasing) both describe vested rights as 'incurring substantial liabilities in a reasonable good faith reliance on the absence of controlling law or regulation, known as vested rights, prior to January 1, 2011', then go on to say 'the following shall constitute sufficient evidence of vested rights...' without definitively listing what counts. RSA 463-A:23 invokes 'constitutionally vested rights' without specifying which. Because the carve-out is doctrinally significant, the imprecise definition matters.
issues.quote: ...incurring substantial liabilities in a reasonable good faith reliance on the absence of controlling law or regulation, known as vested rights, prior to January 1, 2011...

issues.title: 'In due course' used as a timing standard with no fixed meaning
issues.description: The phrase 'in due course' appears as a substantive timing or process trigger in five chapters: RSA 275:48 (employer book-keeping for withheld wages must be recorded 'openly, clearly, and in due course'); RSA 294-E:16 (UCC transferable-records concept of 'holder in due course'); RSA 318:51-f (research organisations must 'destroy all prescription drugs in due course'); RSA 352:7 (art-dealer disclosures, except 'a holder in due course'). The phrase is borrowed from common-law commercial doctrine and is not defined anywhere in the chapters that use it; reuse outside the UCC context (e.g. for drug destruction) imports doctrine that does not obviously fit.
issues.quote: ...shall effectively destroy all prescription drugs in due course by means of conducting routine research operations or disposal by approved methods.

issues.title: Time-reckoning statute privileges Sunday and Saturday under inconsistent labels
issues.description: RSA 21:35 (the statewide statutory-construction time-computation rule) treats deadlines that fall on a 'Saturday, Sunday, or legal holiday' as deemed timely if filed on the next business day. Several proclamation statutes (e.g. RSA 4:13-h Gold Star Mother's Day, RSA 4:13-m Firefighters Memorial Day, RSA 4:13-r Blue Star Mother's Day) call out specific Sundays as named observances. None of these reference each other or use a uniform format; reading them together gives no clean answer to whether a deadline that lands on, say, the 'last Sunday in September' (proclaimed as Gold Star Mother's Day) is a holiday under RSA 21:35.
issues.quote: If the specified date falls on a Saturday, Sunday, or legal holiday, the document or fee shall be deemed timely filed if it is received by the next business day.
